quoteText:
You must lend me these, Basil, I want to
learn them. They are perfectly charming.
subQuotationList: ['You must lend me these, Basil,', 'I want to\nlearn them. They are perfectly charming.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: That entirely depends on how you sit to-day, Dorian.
subQuotationList: ['That entirely depends on how you sit to-day, Dorian.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
Oh, I am tired of sitting, and I don t want a life-sized portrait of
myself,
subQuotationList: ['Oh, I am tired of sitting, and I don t want a life-sized portrait of\nmyself,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
I beg your
pardon, Basil, but I didn t know you had any one with you.
subQuotationList: ['I beg your\npardon, Basil, but I didn t know you had any one with you.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
This is Lord Henry Wotton, Dorian, an old Oxford friend of mine. I
have just been telling him what a capital sitter you were, and now you
have spoiled everything.
subQuotationList: ['This is Lord Henry Wotton, Dorian, an old Oxford friend of mine. I\nhave just been telling him what a capital sitter you were, and now you\nhave spoiled everything.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionTextsList: [['Lord Henry Wotton', 'Henry', 'Wotton', 'Dorian', 'an old Oxford friend of mine', 'him']]

quoteText:
You have not spoiled my pleasure in meeting you, Mr. Gray, My aunt has often
spoken to me about you. You are one of her favourites, and, I am
afraid, one of her victims also.
subQuotationList: ['You have not spoiled my pleasure in meeting you, Mr. Gray,', 'My aunt has often\nspoken to me about you. You are one of her favourites, and, I am\nafraid, one of her victims also.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']], [['Lady Agatha'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lady Agatha'], ['Lady Agatha']]]

quoteText:
I am in Lady Agatha s black books at present, I promised to go to a club in Whitechapel
with her last Tuesday, and I really forgot all about it. We were to
have played a duet together three duets, I believe. I don t know what
she will say to me. I am far too frightened to call.
subQuotationList:
['I am in Lady Agatha s black books at present,', 'I promised to go to a club in Whitechapel
with her last Tuesday, and I really forgot all about it. We were to
have played a duet together three duets, I believe. I don t know what
she will say to me. I am far too frightened to call.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Oh, I will make your peace with my aunt. She is quite devoted to you.
And I don t think it really matters about your not being there. The
audience probably thought it was a duet. When Aunt Agatha sits down to
the piano, she makes quite enough noise for two people.
subQuotationList:
['Oh, I will make your peace with my aunt. She is quite devoted to you.
And I don t think it really matters about your not being there. The
audience probably thought it was a duet. When Aunt Agatha sits down to
the piano, she makes quite enough noise for two people.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Lady Agatha'], ['Lady Agatha'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lady Agatha'], ['Lady Agatha']]]

quoteText: That is very horrid to her, and not very nice to me,
subQuotationList: ['That is very horrid to her, and not very nice to me,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
You are too charming to go in for philanthropy, Mr. Gray far too
charming.
subQuotationList: ['You are too charming to go in for philanthropy, Mr. Gray far too\ncharming.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Harry, I want to finish this picture to-day. Would you think it
awfully rude of me if I asked you to go away?
subQuotationList: ['Harry, I want to finish this picture to-day. Would you think it\nawfully rude of me if I asked you to go away?']
speaker: Basil Hallward
referringExpression: he glanced at him hesitated for a moment and then said

quoteText: Am I to go, Mr. Gray?
subQuotationList: ['Am I to go, Mr. Gray?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Oh, please don t, Lord Henry. I see that Basil is in one of his sulky
moods, and I can t bear him when he sulks. Besides, I want you to tell
me why I should not go in for philanthropy.
subQuotationList: ['Oh, please don t, Lord Henry. I see that Basil is in one of his sulky\nmoods, and I can t bear him when he sulks. Besides, I want you to tell\nme why I should not go in for philanthropy.']
speaker: Dorian Gray
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
I don t know that I shall tell you that, Mr. Gray. It is so tedious a
subject that one would have to talk seriously about it. But I certainly
shall not run away, now that you have asked me to stop. You don t
really mind, Basil, do you? You have often told me that you liked your
sitters to have some one to chat to.
subQuotationList:
['I don t know that I shall tell you that, Mr. Gray. It is so tedious a
subject that one would have to talk seriously about it. But I certainly
shall not run away, now that you have asked me to stop. You don t
really mind, Basil, do you? You have often told me that you liked your
sitters to have some one to chat to.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward']]]

quoteText:
If Dorian wishes it, of course you must stay.
Dorian s whims are laws to everybody, except himself.
subQuotationList: ['If Dorian wishes it, of course you must stay.\nDorian s whims are laws to everybody, except himself.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
You are very pressing, Basil,
but I am afraid I must go. I have promised to meet a man at the
Orleans. Good-bye, Mr. Gray. Come and see me some afternoon in Curzon
Street. I am nearly always at home at five o clock. Write to me when
you are coming. I should be sorry to miss you.
subQuotationList:
['You are very pressing, Basil,
but I am afraid I must go. I have promised to meet a man at the
Orleans. Good-bye, Mr. Gray. Come and see me some afternoon in Curzon
Street. I am nearly always at home at five o clock. Write to me when
you are coming. I should be sorry to miss you.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Basil, if Lord Henry Wotton goes, I shall go,
too. You never open your lips while you are painting, and it is
horribly dull standing on a platform and trying to look pleasant. Ask
him to stay. I insist upon it.
subQuotationList:
['Basil,', 'if Lord Henry Wotton goes, I shall go,
too. You never open your lips while you are painting, and it is
horribly dull standing on a platform and trying to look pleasant. Ask
him to stay. I insist upon it.']
speaker: Dorian Gray
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Basil Hallward']], [['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
Stay, Harry, to oblige Dorian, and to oblige me, It is quite true, I never talk when I
am working, and never listen either, and it must be dreadfully tedious
for my unfortunate sitters. I beg you to stay.
subQuotationList: ['Stay, Harry, to oblige Dorian, and to oblige me,', 'It is quite true, I never talk when I\nam working, and never listen either, and it must be dreadfully tedious\nfor my unfortunate sitters. I beg you to stay.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: But what about my man at the Orleans?
subQuotationList: ['But what about my man at the Orleans?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
I don t think there will be any difficulty about
that. Sit down again, Harry. And now, Dorian, get up on the platform,
and don t move about too much, or pay any attention to what Lord Henry
says. He has a very bad influence over all his friends, with the single
exception of myself.
subQuotationList:
['I don t think there will be any difficulty about
that. Sit down again, Harry. And now, Dorian, get up on the platform,
and don t move about too much, or pay any attention to what Lord Henry
says. He has a very bad influence over all his friends, with the single
exception of myself.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
Have you really a very bad influence, Lord
Henry? As bad as Basil says?
subQuotationList: ['Have you really a very bad influence, Lord\nHenry? As bad as Basil says?']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
There is no such thing as a good influence, Mr. Gray. All influence is
immoral immoral from the scientific point of view.
subQuotationList: ['There is no such thing as a good influence, Mr. Gray. All influence is\nimmoral immoral from the scientific point of view.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Why?
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Because to influence a person is to give him one s own soul. He does
not think his natural thoughts, or burn with his natural passions. His
virtues are not real to him. His sins, if there are such things as
sins, are borrowed. He becomes an echo of some one else s music, an
actor of a part that has not been written for him. The aim of life is
self-development. To realize one s nature perfectly that is what each
of us is here for. People are afraid of themselves, nowadays. They have
forgotten the highest of all duties, the duty that one owes to one s
self. Of course, they are charitable. They feed the hungry and clothe
the beggar. But their own souls starve, and are naked. Courage has gone
out of our race. Perhaps we never really had it. The terror of society,
which is the basis of morals, the terror of God, which is the secret of
religion these are the two things that govern us. And yet
subQuotationList:
['Because to influence a person is to give him one s own soul. He does
not think his natural thoughts, or burn with his natural passions. His

out of our race. Perhaps we never really had it. The terror of society,
which is the basis of morals, the terror of God, which is the secret of
religion these are the two things that govern us. And yet']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Just turn your head a little more to the right, Dorian, like a good
boy,
subQuotationList: ['Just turn your head a little more to the right, Dorian, like a good\nboy,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
And yet, I believe that if one man
were to live out his life fully and completely, were to give form to
every feeling, expression to every thought, reality to every dream I
believe that the world would gain such a fresh impulse of joy that we
would forget all the maladies of medi valism, and return to the
Hellenic ideal to something finer, richer than the Hellenic ideal, it
may be. But the bravest man amongst us is afraid of himself. The
mutilation of the savage has its tragic survival in the self-denial
that mars our lives. We are punished for our refusals. Every impulse
that we strive to strangle broods in the mind and poisons us. The body
sins once, and has done with its sin, for action is a mode of
purification. Nothing remains then but the recollection of a pleasure,
or the luxury of a regret. The only way to get rid of a temptation is
to yield to it. Resist it, and your soul grows sick with longing for
the things it has forbidden to itself, with desire for what its
monstrous laws have made monstrous and unlawful. It has been said that
the great events of the world take place in the brain. It is in the
brain, and the brain only, that the great sins of the world take place
also. You, Mr. Gray, you yourself, with your rose-red youth and your
rose-white boyhood, you have had passions that have made you afraid,
thoughts that have filled you with terror, day-dreams and sleeping
dreams whose mere memory might stain your cheek with shame

mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText:
Stop! stop! you bewilder me. I don t know what
to say. There is some answer to you, but I cannot find it. Don t speak.
Let me think. Or, rather, let me try not to think.
subQuotationList: ['Stop!', 'stop! you bewilder me. I don t know what\nto say. There is some answer to you, but I cannot find it. Don t speak.\nLet me think. Or, rather, let me try not to think.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Basil, I am tired of standing, I must go
out and sit in the garden. The air is stifling here.
subQuotationList: ['Basil, I am tired of standing,', 'I must go\nout and sit in the garden. The air is stifling here.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
My dear fellow, I am so sorry. When I am painting, I can t think of
anything else. But you never sat better. You were perfectly still. And
I have caught the effect I wanted the half-parted lips and the bright
look in the eyes. I don t know what Harry has been saying to you, but
he has certainly made you have the most wonderful expression. I suppose
he has been paying you compliments. You mustn t believe a word that he
says.
subQuotationList:
['My dear fellow, I am so sorry. When I am painting, I can t think of
anything else. But you never sat better. You were perfectly still. And
I have caught the effect I wanted the half-parted lips and the bright
look in the eyes. I don t know what Harry has been saying to you, but
he has certainly made you have the most wonderful expression. I suppose
he has been paying you compliments. You mustn t believe a word that he
says.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
He has certainly not been paying me compliments. Perhaps that is the
reason that I don t believe anything he has told me.
subQuotationList: ['He has certainly not been paying me compliments. Perhaps that is the\nreason that I don t believe anything he has told me.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
You know you believe it all, I will go out to the garden with you. It is
horribly hot in the studio. Basil, let us have something iced to drink,
something with strawberries in it.
subQuotationList: ['You know you believe it all,', 'I will go out to the garden with you. It is\nhorribly hot in the studio. Basil, let us have something iced to drink,\nsomething with strawberries in it.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
Certainly, Harry. Just touch the bell, and when Parker comes I will
tell him what you want. I have got to work up this background, so I
will join you later on. Don t keep Dorian too long. I have never been
in better form for painting than I am to-day. This is going to be my
masterpiece. It is my masterpiece as it stands.
subQuotationList:
['Certainly, Harry. Just touch the bell, and when Parker comes I will
tell him what you want. I have got to work up this background, so I
will join you later on. Don t keep Dorian too long. I have never been
in better form for painting than I am to-day. This is going to be my
masterpiece. It is my masterpiece as it stands.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
You are quite right to do that, Nothing can cure the soul but the senses, just as nothing can cure the
senses but the soul.
subQuotationList: ['You are quite right to do that,', 'Nothing can cure the soul but the senses, just as nothing can cure the\nsenses but the soul.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Yes, that is one of the great secrets of
life to cure the soul by means of the senses, and the senses by means
of the soul. You are a wonderful creation. You know more than you think
you know, just as you know less than you want to know.
subQuotationList:
['Yes,', 'that is one of the great secrets of
life to cure the soul by means of the senses, and the senses by means
of the soul. You are a wonderful creation. You know more than you think
you know, just as you know less than you want to know.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText:
Let us go and sit in the shade, Parker has brought
out the drinks, and if you stay any longer in this glare, you will be
quite spoiled, and Basil will never paint you again. You really must
not allow yourself to become sunburnt. It would be unbecoming.
subQuotationList:
['Let us go and sit in the shade,', 'Parker has brought
out the drinks, and if you stay any longer in this glare, you will be
quite spoiled, and Basil will never paint you again. You really must
not allow yourself to become sunburnt. It would be unbecoming.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry']], [['Parker'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: What can it matter?
subQuotationList: ['What can it matter?']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: It should matter everything to you, Mr. Gray.
subQuotationList: ['It should matter everything to you, Mr. Gray.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Why?
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Because you have the most marvellous youth, and youth is the one thing
worth having.
subQuotationList: ['Because you have the most marvellous youth, and youth is the one thing\nworth having.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I don t feel that, Lord Henry.
subQuotationList: ['I don t feel that, Lord Henry.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
No, you don t feel it now. Some day, when you are old and wrinkled and
ugly, when thought has seared your forehead with its lines, and passion
branded your lips with its hideous fires, you will feel it, you will
feel it terribly. Now, wherever you go, you charm the world. Will it
always be so? ... You have a wonderfully beautiful face, Mr. Gray.
Don t frown. You have. And beauty is a form of genius is higher,
indeed, than genius, as it needs no explanation. It is of the great
facts of the world, like sunlight, or spring-time, or the reflection in
dark waters of that silver shell we call the moon. It cannot be
questioned. It has its divine right of sovereignty. It makes princes of
those who have it. You smile? Ah! when you have lost it you won t
smile.... People say sometimes that beauty is only superficial. That
may be so, but at least it is not so superficial as thought is. To me,
beauty is the wonder of wonders. It is only shallow people who do not
judge by appearances. The true mystery of the world is the visible, not
the invisible.... Yes, Mr. Gray, the gods have been good to you. But
what the gods give they quickly take away. You have only a few years in
which to live really, perfectly, and fully. When your youth goes, your
beauty will go with it, and then you will suddenly discover that there
are no triumphs left for you, or have to content yourself with those
mean triumphs that the memory of your past will make more bitter than
defeats. Every month as it wanes brings you nearer to something
dreadful. Time is jealous of you, and wars against your lilies and your
roses. You will become sallow, and hollow-cheeked, and dull-eyed. You
will suffer horribly.... Ah! realize your youth while you have it.
Don t squander the gold of your days, listening to the tedious, trying
to improve the hopeless failure, or giving away your life to the
ignorant, the common, and the vulgar. These are the sickly aims, the
false ideals, of our age. Live! Live the wonderful life that is in you!
Let nothing be lost upon you. Be always searching for new sensations.
Be afraid of nothing.... A new Hedonism that is what our century wants.
You might be its visible symbol. With your personality there is nothing
you could not do. The world belongs to you for a season.... The moment
I met you I saw that you were quite unconscious of what you really are,
of what you really might be. There was so much in you that charmed me
that I felt I must tell you something about yourself. I thought how
tragic it would be if you were wasted. For there is such a little time
that your youth will last such a little time. The common hill-flowers
wither, but they blossom again. The laburnum will be as yellow next
June as it is now. In a month there will be purple stars on the
clematis, and year after year the green night of its leaves will hold
its purple stars. But we never get back our youth. The pulse of joy
that beats in us at twenty becomes sluggish. Our limbs fail, our senses
rot. We degenerate into hideous puppets, haunted by the memory of the
passions of which we were too much afraid, and the exquisite
temptations that we had not the courage to yield to. Youth! Youth!
There is absolutely nothing in the world but youth!

quoteText:
I am waiting, Do come in. The light is quite perfect, and
you can bring your drinks.
subQuotationList: ['I am waiting,', 'Do come in. The light is quite perfect, and\nyou can bring your drinks.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: You are glad you have met me, Mr. Gray,
subQuotationList: ['You are glad you have met me, Mr. Gray,']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Yes, I am glad now. I wonder shall I always be glad?
subQuotationList: ['Yes, I am glad now. I wonder shall I always be glad?']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Always! That is a dreadful word. It makes me shudder when I hear it.
Women are so fond of using it. They spoil every romance by trying to
make it last for ever. It is a meaningless word, too. The only
difference between a caprice and a lifelong passion is that the caprice
lasts a little longer.
subQuotationList:
['Always! That is a dreadful word. It makes me shudder when I hear it.
Women are so fond of using it. They spoil every romance by trying to
make it last for ever. It is a meaningless word, too. The only
difference between a caprice and a lifelong passion is that the caprice
lasts a little longer.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: In that case, let our friendship be a caprice,
subQuotationList: ['In that case, let our friendship be a caprice,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
It is quite
finished,
subQuotationList: ['It is quite\nfinished,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
My dear fellow, I congratulate you most warmly, It is the
finest portrait of modern times. Mr. Gray, come over and look at
yourself.
subQuotationList: ['My dear fellow, I congratulate you most warmly,', 'It is the\nfinest portrait of modern times. Mr. Gray, come over and look at\nyourself.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Is it really finished?
subQuotationList: ['Is it really finished?']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Quite finished, And you have sat splendidly
to-day. I am awfully obliged to you.
subQuotationList: ['Quite finished,', 'And you have sat splendidly\nto-day. I am awfully obliged to you.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
That is entirely due to me, Isn t it, Mr.
Gray?
subQuotationList: ['That is entirely due to me,', 'Isn t it, Mr.\nGray?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Don t you like it?
subQuotationList: ['Don t you like it?']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
Of course he likes it, Who wouldn t like it? It is
one of the greatest things in modern art. I will give you anything you
like to ask for it. I must have it.
subQuotationList: ['Of course he likes it,', 'Who wouldn t like it? It is\none of the greatest things in modern art. I will give you anything you\nlike to ask for it. I must have it.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: It is not my property, Harry.
subQuotationList: ['It is not my property, Harry.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: Whose property is it?
subQuotationList: ['Whose property is it?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Dorian s, of course,
subQuotationList: ['Dorian s, of course,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: He is a very lucky fellow.
subQuotationList: ['He is a very lucky fellow.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
How sad it is! How sad it is! I shall grow old, and horrible, and
dreadful. But this picture will remain always young. It will never be
older than this particular day of June.... If it were only the other
way! If it were I who was to be always young, and the picture that was
to grow old! For that for that I would give everything! Yes, there is
nothing in the whole world I would not give! I would give my soul for
that!
subQuotationList:
['How sad it is!', 'How sad it is! I shall grow old, and horrible, and
dreadful. But this picture will remain always young. It will never be
older than this particular day of June.... If it were only the other
way! If it were I who was to be always young, and the picture that was
to grow old! For that for that I would give everything! Yes, there is
nothing in the whole world I would not give! I would give my soul for
that!']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: You would hardly care for such an arrangement, Basil, It would be rather hard lines on your work.
subQuotationList: ['You would hardly care for such an arrangement, Basil,', 'It would be rather hard lines on your work.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I should object very strongly, Harry,
subQuotationList: ['I should object very strongly, Harry,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
I believe you would, Basil. You
like your art better than your friends. I am no more to you than a
green bronze figure. Hardly as much, I dare say.
subQuotationList: ['I believe you would, Basil. You\nlike your art better than your friends. I am no more to you than a\ngreen bronze figure. Hardly as much, I dare say.']
speaker: Dorian Gray
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward']]]

quoteText:
Yes, I am less to you than your ivory Hermes or your
silver Faun. You will like them always. How long will you like me? Till
I have my first wrinkle, I suppose. I know, now, that when one loses
one s good looks, whatever they may be, one loses everything. Your
picture has taught me that. Lord Henry Wotton is perfectly right. Youth
is the only thing worth having. When I find that I am growing old, I
shall kill myself.
subQuotationList:
['Yes,', 'I am less to you than your ivory Hermes or your
silver Faun. You will like them always. How long will you like me? Till
I have my first wrinkle, I suppose. I know, now, that when one loses
one s good looks, whatever they may be, one loses everything. Your
picture has taught me that. Lord Henry Wotton is perfectly right. Youth
is the only thing worth having. When I find that I am growing old, I
shall kill myself.']
speaker: Dorian Gray
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText:
Dorian! Dorian! don t talk like that. I have never had such a friend as you, and I
shall never have such another. You are not jealous of material things,
are you? you who are finer than any of them!
subQuotationList: ['Dorian! Dorian!', 'don t talk like that. I have never had such a friend as you, and I\nshall never have such another. You are not jealous of material things,\nare you? you who are finer than any of them!']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']], [['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText:
I am jealous of everything whose beauty does not die. I am jealous of
the portrait you have painted of me. Why should it keep what I must
lose? Every moment that passes takes something from me and gives
something to it. Oh, if it were only the other way! If the picture
could change, and I could be always what I am now! Why did you paint
it? It will mock me some day mock me horribly!
subQuotationList:
['I am jealous of everything whose beauty does not die. I am jealous of
the portrait you have painted of me. Why should it keep what I must
lose? Every moment that passes takes something from me and gives
something to it. Oh, if it were only the other way! If the picture
could change, and I could be always what I am now! Why did you paint
it? It will mock me some day mock me horribly!']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: This is your doing, Harry,
subQuotationList: ['This is your doing, Harry,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
It is the real Dorian Gray that is
all.
subQuotationList: ['It is the real Dorian Gray that is\nall.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: It is not.
subQuotationList: ['It is not.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: If it is not, what have I to do with it?
subQuotationList: ['If it is not, what have I to do with it?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: You should have gone away when I asked you,
subQuotationList: ['You should have gone away when I asked you,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: I stayed when you asked me,
subQuotationList: ['I stayed when you asked me,']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Harry, I can t quarrel with my two best friends at once, but between
you both you have made me hate the finest piece of work I have ever
done, and I will destroy it. What is it but canvas and colour? I will
not let it come across our three lives and mar them.
subQuotationList:
['Harry, I can t quarrel with my two best friends at once, but between
you both you have made me hate the finest piece of work I have ever
done, and I will destroy it. What is it but canvas and colour? I will
not let it come across our three lives and mar them.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Basil Hallward'], ['Lord Henry']]]

quoteText: Don t, Basil, don t! It would be murder!
subQuotationList: ['Don t, Basil, don t!', 'It would be murder!']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
I am glad you appreciate my work at last, Dorian, I never thought you
would.
subQuotationList: ['I am glad you appreciate my work at last, Dorian,', 'I never thought you\nwould.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
Appreciate it? I am in love with it, Basil. It is part of myself. I
feel that.
subQuotationList: ['Appreciate it? I am in love with it, Basil. It is part of myself. I\nfeel that.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Well, as soon as you are dry, you shall be varnished, and framed, and
sent home. Then you can do what you like with yourself.
subQuotationList: ['Well, as soon as you are dry, you shall be varnished, and framed, and\nsent home. Then you can do what you like with yourself.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
You will have tea, of
course, Dorian? And so will you, Harry? Or do you object to such simple
pleasures?
subQuotationList: ['You will have tea, of\ncourse, Dorian? And so will you, Harry? Or do you object to such simple\npleasures?']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
I adore simple pleasures, They are the last refuge
of the complex. But I don t like scenes, except on the stage. What
absurd fellows you are, both of you! I wonder who it was defined man as
a rational animal. It was the most premature definition ever given. Man
is many things, but he is not rational. I am glad he is not, after
all though I wish you chaps would not squabble over the picture. You
had much better let me have it, Basil. This silly boy doesn t really
want it, and I really do.
subQuotationList:
['I adore simple pleasures,', 'They are the last refuge
of the complex. But I don t like scenes, except on the stage. What
absurd fellows you are, both of you! I wonder who it was defined man as
a rational animal. It was the most premature definition ever given. Man
is many things, but he is not rational. I am glad he is not, after
all though I wish you chaps would not squabble over the picture. You
had much better let me have it, Basil. This silly boy doesn t really
want it, and I really do.']
speaker: Lord Henry
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Dorian Gray', 'Basil Hallward'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward', 'Dorian Gray'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Basil Hallward'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: If you let any one have it but me, Basil, I shall never forgive you! and I don t allow people to call me a silly boy.
subQuotationList: ['If you let any one have it but me, Basil, I shall never forgive you!', 'and I don t allow people to call me a silly boy.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
You know the picture is yours, Dorian. I gave it to you before it
existed.
subQuotationList: ['You know the picture is yours, Dorian. I gave it to you before it\nexisted.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
And you know you have been a little silly, Mr. Gray, and that you
don t really object to being reminded that you are extremely young.
subQuotationList: ['And you know you have been a little silly, Mr. Gray, and that you\ndon t really object to being reminded that you are extremely young.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I should have objected very strongly this morning, Lord Henry.
subQuotationList: ['I should have objected very strongly this morning, Lord Henry.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: Ah! this morning! You have lived since then.
subQuotationList: ['Ah! this morning! You have lived since then.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Let us go to the theatre to-night, There is sure to
be something on, somewhere. I have promised to dine at White s, but it
is only with an old friend, so I can send him a wire to say that I am
ill, or that I am prevented from coming in consequence of a subsequent
engagement. I think that would be a rather nice excuse: it would have
all the surprise of candour.
subQuotationList:
['Let us go to the theatre to-night,', 'There is sure to
be something on, somewhere. I have promised to dine at White s, but it
is only with an old friend, so I can send him a wire to say that I am
ill, or that I am prevented from coming in consequence of a subsequent
engagement. I think that would be a rather nice excuse: it would have
all the surprise of candour.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: It is such a bore putting on one s dress-clothes, And, when one has them on, they are so horrid.
subQuotationList: ['It is such a bore putting on one s dress-clothes,', 'And, when one has them on, they are so horrid.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
Yes, the costume of the nineteenth
century is detestable. It is so sombre, so depressing. Sin is the only
real colour-element left in modern life.
subQuotationList: ['Yes,', 'the costume of the nineteenth\ncentury is detestable. It is so sombre, so depressing. Sin is the only\nreal colour-element left in modern life.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: You really must not say things like that before Dorian, Harry.
subQuotationList: ['You really must not say things like that before Dorian, Harry.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
Before which Dorian? The one who is pouring out tea for us, or the one
in the picture?
subQuotationList: ['Before which Dorian? The one who is pouring out tea for us, or the one\nin the picture?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Before either.
subQuotationList: ['Before either.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: I should like to come to the theatre with you, Lord Henry,
subQuotationList: ['I should like to come to the theatre with you, Lord Henry,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: Then you shall come; and you will come, too, Basil, won t you?
subQuotationList: ['Then you shall come; and you will come, too, Basil, won t you?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I can t, really. I would sooner not. I have a lot of work to do.
subQuotationList: ['I can t, really. I would sooner not. I have a lot of work to do.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: Well, then, you and I will go alone, Mr. Gray.
subQuotationList: ['Well, then, you and I will go alone, Mr. Gray.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I should like that awfully.
subQuotationList: ['I should like that awfully.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: I shall stay with the real Dorian,
subQuotationList: ['I shall stay with the real Dorian,']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: Is it the real Dorian? Am I really like that?
subQuotationList: ['Is it the real Dorian?', 'Am I really like that?']
speaker: Dorian Gray
referringExpression: cried the original of the portrait

quoteText: Yes; you are just like that.
subQuotationList: ['Yes; you are just like that.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: How wonderful, Basil!
subQuotationList: ['How wonderful, Basil!']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: At least you are like it in appearance. But it will never alter, That is something.
subQuotationList: ['At least you are like it in appearance. But it will never alter,', 'That is something.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
What a fuss people make about fidelity! Why,
even in love it is purely a question for physiology. It has nothing to
do with our own will. Young men want to be faithful, and are not; old
men want to be faithless, and cannot: that is all one can say.
subQuotationList:
['What a fuss people make about fidelity!', 'Why,
even in love it is purely a question for physiology. It has nothing to
do with our own will. Young men want to be faithful, and are not; old
men want to be faithless, and cannot: that is all one can say.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText:
Don t go to the theatre to-night, Dorian, Stop and
dine with me.
subQuotationList: ['Don t go to the theatre to-night, Dorian,', 'Stop and\ndine with me.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: I can t, Basil.
subQuotationList: ['I can t, Basil.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: Why?
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: Because I have promised Lord Henry Wotton to go with him.
subQuotationList: ['Because I have promised Lord Henry Wotton to go with him.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
He won t like you the better for keeping your promises. He always
breaks his own. I beg you not to go.
subQuotationList: ['He won t like you the better for keeping your promises. He always\nbreaks his own. I beg you not to go.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Dorian Gray'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: I entreat you.
subQuotationList: ['I entreat you.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: I must go, Basil,
subQuotationList: ['I must go, Basil,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText:
Very well, It is rather late, and, as you have to dress, you had better
lose no time. Good-bye, Harry. Good-bye, Dorian. Come and see me soon.
Come to-morrow.
subQuotationList: ['Very well,', 'It is rather late, and, as you have to dress, you had better\nlose no time. Good-bye, Harry. Good-bye, Dorian. Come and see me soon.\nCome to-morrow.']
speaker: Basil Hallward
mentionEntitiesList: [[], [['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray', 'Lord Henry'], ['Lord Henry'], ['Dorian Gray']]]

quoteText: Certainly.
subQuotationList: ['Certainly.']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: You won t forget?
subQuotationList: ['You won t forget?']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: No, of course not,
subQuotationList: ['No, of course not,']
speaker: Dorian Gray

quoteText: And ... Harry!
subQuotationList: ['And ... Harry!']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: Yes, Basil?
subQuotationList: ['Yes, Basil?']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: Remember what I asked you, when we were in the garden this morning.
subQuotationList: ['Remember what I asked you, when we were in the garden this morning.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText: I have forgotten it.
subQuotationList: ['I have forgotten it.']
speaker: Lord Henry

quoteText: I trust you.
subQuotationList: ['I trust you.']
speaker: Basil Hallward

quoteText:
I wish I could trust myself, Come, Mr.
Gray, my hansom is outside, and I can drop you at your own place.
Good-bye, Basil. It has been a most interesting afternoon.
subQuotationList: ['I wish I could trust myself,', 'Come, Mr.\nGray, my hansom is outside, and I can drop you at your own place.\nGood-bye, Basil. It has been a most interesting afternoon.']
speaker: Lord Henry
